test_cases.question: What crops grow best in Bihar during monsoon season?
test_cases.reference_answer: During monsoon season in Bihar, rice (paddy) is the primary crop. Other suitable crops include maize, pulses like arhar and moong, vegetables like cucumber and bottle gourd, and oilseeds.

test_cases.question: How can I prevent leaf blight in wheat?
test_cases.reference_answer: To prevent leaf blight in wheat: 1) Use resistant varieties, 2) Practice crop rotation, 3) Treat seeds with fungicide, 4) Ensure proper spacing for air circulation, 5) Apply fungicide sprays if needed.

test_cases.question: When is the best time to plant tomatoes?
test_cases.reference_answer: Tomatoes grow best when planted after the last frost. In India, the ideal planting times are: Kharif season (June-July) in northern regions, and Rabi season (October-November) in southern regions.

test_cases.question: What are the current market prices for wheat?
test_cases.reference_answer: Market prices vary by region and quality. Check local mandi rates or the government MSP (Minimum Support Price). Farmers should also consider transportation costs and market demand.

test_cases.question: How do I get rid of aphids on my cotton plants?
test_cases.reference_answer: For aphid control: 1) Use neem oil spray, 2) Introduce natural predators like ladybugs, 3) Use insecticidal soap, 4) Apply chemical pesticides as last resort, 5) Monitor regularly for early detection.

test_cases.question: How can I improve soil fertility?
test_cases.reference_answer: Improve soil fertility by: 1) Adding organic compost, 2) Using green manure crops, 3) Practicing crop rotation, 4) Applying farmyard manure, 5) Balancing NPK fertilizers based on soil testing.

test_cases.question: What is drip irrigation and how does it help?
test_cases.reference_answer: Drip irrigation delivers water directly to plant roots through tubes. Benefits: 90% water efficiency, reduced weed growth, lower disease risk, uniform water distribution, and reduced labor costs.

test_cases.question: How much urea should I apply for rice crop?
test_cases.reference_answer: For rice, apply 120-150 kg urea per hectare in 2-3 splits: 1/3 at planting, 1/3 at tillering (20-25 days), and 1/3 at panicle initiation (45-50 days). Adjust based on soil test results.

test_cases.question: How can I store grains to prevent spoilage?
test_cases.reference_answer: For grain storage: 1) Dry to 12% moisture or less, 2) Use clean, airtight containers, 3) Keep in cool, dry place, 4) Use hermetic bags, 5) Monitor for pests, 6) Consider using neem leaves as natural pesticide.

test_cases.question: What are the benefits of organic farming?
test_cases.reference_answer: Organic farming benefits: 1) Healthier soil and crops, 2) No chemical residues, 3) Better for environment, 4) Higher market prices, 5) Sustainable long-term, 6) Improves biodiversity.

test_cases.question: What government schemes are available for farmers?
test_cases.reference_answer: Major schemes: 1) PM-KISAN (income support), 2) PM Fasal Bima Yojana (crop insurance), 3) Kisan Credit Card (easy loans), 4) Soil Health Card, 5) Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (irrigation).

test_cases.question: What nutrients does my maize crop need?
test_cases.reference_answer: Maize requires balanced NPK: 120-150 kg Nitrogen, 60-80 kg Phosphorus, 40-60 kg Potassium per hectare. Also needs Zinc (25 kg ZnSO4) and Sulphur. Apply in splits for better efficiency.

test_cases.question: Why is crop rotation important?
test_cases.reference_answer: Crop rotation: 1) Breaks pest and disease cycles, 2) Improves soil fertility, 3) Reduces weed pressure, 4) Balances nutrient usage, 5) Increases crop yields, 6) Reduces dependency on chemicals.

test_cases.question: How can farming reduce carbon footprint?
test_cases.reference_answer: Reduce carbon footprint by: 1) No-till farming, 2) Cover cropping, 3) Organic farming, 4) Efficient water use, 5) Renewable energy, 6) Reduced fertilizer use, 7) Agroforestry.

test_cases.question: What is the difference between Kharif and Rabi crops?
test_cases.reference_answer: Kharif crops are sown in June-July (monsoon) and harvested in September-October (rice, maize, cotton). Rabi crops are sown in October-November (winter) and harvested in March-April (wheat, mustard, chickpea).
